transcript: Good evening. I reviewed your chest x-ray and this looks like community-acquired pneumonia. In the emergency department we will give you ceftriaksone, and after discharge I want you to continue azithro and mycin for the next several days. Please come back right away if your breathing gets worse or if you develop persistent high fever.

This patient is in septic shock with persistent hypotension despite fluids. Start norepi and phrine immediately and titrate to maintain mean arterial pressure above sixty-five. After blood cultures, begin vancomysin and broad gram-negative coverage. If lactate keeps rising, call the intensive care unit team again.

Your urine studies show significant hematuria and proteinuria, and the nephrology team is worried about rapidly progressive glomerulo nephritis. We may need a kidney biopsy, and if the diagnosis is confirmed we will likely start pulse methilprednisolone followed by additional immunosuppressive treatment depending on the pathology results.

The platelet count has fallen again, and this degree of thrombo cytopenia raises concern for an immune-mediated process. If steroids fail, we may escalate to rituximub after discussing risks, benefits, and infection monitoring. Please seek urgent care for any bleeding, black stools, or new neurologic symptoms.

Given the low CD4 count and diffuse bilateral infiltrates, I am highly concerned about pneumo cystis jiro vet see pneumonia. We should begin sulfa meth oxazole and trimethoprim immediately and add corticosteroids if the oxygenation worsens. You will also need close infectious disease follow-up after discharge.

The patient likely has pyelonefritis based on flank pain, fever, and the urinalysis. I want to start cipro and floxacin today and send the urine culture. If she develops vomiting or worsening back pain, she should come back to the emergency department immediately.

Your symptoms and MRI findings are concerning for multiple sklerosis. We usually treat acute relapses with high-dose methilprednisolone, and depending on the pattern of disease we may discuss ocreli and zumab as a disease-modifying therapy later on.

Given the crushing chest pain and the ST elevations, this is most consistent with an acute myokardial infarction. We are activating the cath lab now and will give aspirin, heparin, and clopi and dogrel as soon as possible unless there is a contraindication.

Your blood counts and bone marrow biopsy are concerning for acute promyelo and cytic leukemia. This subtype can be associated with dangerous coagulopathy, so we need to start all trans retinoic acid immediately and involve hematology right away.

The rash distribution and skin biopsy are suspicious for leuko cytoclastic vasculitis. We will check inflammatory markers, kidney function, and urine studies. If this worsens, we may start predni sone while we continue the diagnostic workup.

The colonoscopy findings are most consistent with ulcerative colitus. I am going to start mesala and mine and arrange close gastroenterology follow-up. If you develop severe abdominal pain, bleeding, or dehydration, return immediately.

You are in atrial fibrilation, and because of your stroke risk we should start apixa and ban unless there is a bleeding contraindication. I also want to arrange an echocardiogram and outpatient cardiology follow-up.

The CT scan suggests perforated appendicitis, so we are starting broad-spectrum coverage with piperacillin tazo and bactam before surgery. If your pain worsens or you become more lightheaded, tell the nurse immediately.

The severe headaches, jaw claudication, and elevated inflammatory markers raise concern for giant cell arteritus. Because of the risk of vision loss, we should begin predni and sone today and arrange temporal artery evaluation.

Based on the bronchoscopy and tissue sampling, the pulmonology team is worried about sarkoydosis. We may start methotrexate if the disease becomes steroid-refractory, but for now we will monitor symptoms and lung function closely.

The blood culture grew methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, so we are narrowing to dapto and mycin because the kidney function is worsening. Please let us know right away if you develop muscle pain or weakness.

The pattern of proximal muscle weakness and elevated creatine kinase makes inflammatory myopathi likely, and the rheumatologist is also considering dermato myositis. We may need MRI and muscle biopsy depending on how the labs evolve.

Your blood counts are stable today, but the thrombocytopenia will still need repeat monitoring. We are not changing the methylprednisolone dose, and the nephrology team still believes this is glomerulonephritis.

Given the low blood pressure in septic shock, we are continuing norepinephrine and vasopressin while watching your urine output and lactate closely. The infectious disease team agrees with vancomycin for now.

The CT chest findings and low CD4 count are concerning for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, so we started sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim and corticosteroids. We will monitor oxygen requirements and electrolytes carefully.

The bone marrow biopsy confirmed acute promyelocytic leukemia, and hematology has already started all-trans retinoic acid. Because of the coagulopathy risk, the patient will remain in a monitored setting.

The skin biopsy was most consistent with leukocytoclastic vasculitis, and we may use prednisone if the renal studies worsen. For now, continue close follow-up and return if you notice blood in the urine.
